title: Upstream oil and gas 2026 outlook: half-time report | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Wood Mackenzie's H1 2026 upstream review finds oil and gas companies banking a cash windfall from Middle East conflict-driven prices (Brent averaging at least US$15/bbl above the planned ~US$60/bbl) while maintaining capital discipline, deferring maintenance rather than boosting investment, even as portfolio renewal pressure and rising exploration risk appetite build ahead of a possible new upstream M&A wave in 2027.

themes.theme: Capital discipline holds despite price windfall

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie expected a second consecutive year of slight decline in global upstream development spend in 2026, and this held true despite the cash windfall from higher oil and gas prices accelerating company deleveraging.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Confirms upstream operators are prioritizing balance-sheet repair and shareholder returns over new investment even amid a price windfall, a bullish signal for capital discipline and free cash flow generation in the sector.

insights.claim: Operators have mainly captured the 2026 price upside through maintenance deferral, optimisation and 'capex-light' activities rather than major new investment, raising the open question of whether this capital discipline will hold through H2 2026 and into 2027.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Investors should watch for signs of capital discipline breaking down in H2 2026/2027 as a signal that upstream capex growth (and associated service-sector demand) could reaccelerate.

themes.theme: Margin windfall from the Middle East conflict

insights.claim: Companies had planned 2026 budgets around a Brent Crude price of around US$60/barrel, but actual prices could average at least US$15/barrel higher while costs have remained relatively stable, meaning most upstream firms will be net beneficiaries of higher prices even as some Gulf operators lose output.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: A $15/bbl-plus positive surprise versus budget with flat costs implies materially higher-than-planned free cash flow across the upstream sector in 2026, supportive of dividends, buybacks and debt paydown.
insights.locator: section 2, Margins have exceeded all expectations

insights.claim: Firms operating in the Gulf have lost output due to the Middle East conflict, but nearly all will still be net beneficiaries of the resulting higher oil and gas prices.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Even operators with direct Gulf production exposure to conflict-related disruption are financially better off on net, suggesting broad-based sector margin support rather than a mixed winners/losers picture.
insights.locator: section 2, Margins have exceeded all expectations

themes.theme: Portfolio renewal pressure and rising exploration risk appetite

insights.claim: Long-term portfolio renewal and opportunity capture were flagged at the start of 2026 as key priorities for upstream operators, and this urgency has intensified as many companies face large production declines from existing assets over the next decade.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Structural decline in existing asset bases is forcing upstream companies toward new resource capture, a driver of increased M&A and exploration activity investors should anticipate.
insights.locator: section 3, Geopolitical risk adds to portfolio renewal pressure

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict has highlighted the concentration risk of upstream portfolios focused on geopolitically unstable regions, adding urgency to companies' portfolio renewal and diversification efforts.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Geopolitical concentration risk is becoming an explicit factor in upstream portfolio strategy, likely to drive incremental investment toward the Americas, Asia and international gas/unconventional plays.
insights.locator: section 3, Geopolitical risk adds to portfolio renewal pressure

insights.claim: Upstream firms' appetite for exploration risk is rising, with companies adding acreage, taking on more exposure and positioning earlier, even though exploration budgets have so far only risen slightly.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: A shift toward earlier-stage, higher-risk exploration positioning ahead of a full budget increase suggests upstream companies are quietly re-rating their risk tolerance, a leading indicator for future exploration capex growth.
insights.locator: section 5, Exploration risk appetite is rising

themes.theme: M&A outlook and the Middle East's enduring role

insights.claim: Upstream M&A deal count in H1 2026 was within touching distance of recent highs despite market uncertainty and price volatility, with a lack of inventory driving strong M&A appetite even as volatility hampers dealmaking.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Near-record H1 2026 deal counts despite volatility signal that inventory scarcity is now a stronger driver of upstream M&A than price stability, supporting expectations of continued or accelerating deal flow into 2027.
insights.locator: section 4, Will 2027 see a new wave of upstream M&A?

insights.claim: The Middle East conflict may divert some upstream investment toward the Americas, Asia and international gas/unconventional opportunities, yet Wood Mackenzie still expects the Middle East to play a critical role in the longer-term portfolio renewal plans of the world's largest oil and gas companies.
insights.speaker: Fraser McKay
insights.so_what: Despite near-term capital diversion away from the Middle East, the region remains structurally indispensable to Majors' long-term resource base, meaning any conflict-driven pullback is likely tactical rather than permanent.
insights.locator: section 6, Why the Middle East is indispensable to upstream long-term
